none: A man speaks to a woman offscreen.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: Reynolds’s confession about being visited by his mother from the afterlife in dreams and smelling her scent subtly introduces a key theme of Phantom Thread. Although he will never admit it, he seeks a maternal figure who can replace his mother and his love for her. It’s telling that these visions and smells immediately precede Alma’s introduction into Reynolds’s life, as if his mother has been preparing him for the woman who will soon take him by surprise. For all these reasons, Reynolds’s love for Alma is deeply complicated—a blend of maternal need and erotic lust.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: Reynolds believes that it’s “comforting to think that the dead are watching over the living...not spooky at all.” Unsurprisingly, then, his mother will appear to him in a vision at his country house during his first poisoning. This idea of a haunting is central to understanding Phantom Thread as the past looms over Reynolds and Alma throughout the film in various forms. There are allusions to his time in the war, and she, of course, is an immigrant who may or may not have also been a refugee; Reynolds’s mother is also a prime “phantom” that dictates much of his passion and confusion. He will likewise speak of the “air of quiet death” that looms over his house when he grows incredibly frustrated with Alma’s attentions after their wedding. The film, then, is an exploration of how Reynolds’s House of Woodcock has become a house of the dead and how Alma brings new life to it—through untraditional means.
